coredata.dc:title: A sequential dual-lock strategy for generation of room-temperature phosphorescence of boron doped carbon dots for dynamic anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Li
Zhang, Qi
Huang, Yueyue
Luo, Canxia
Quan, Zongyan
Li, Hongjuan
Sun, Shiguo
Xu, Yongqian
coredata.dc:description: Stimuli-responsive materials with dynamically switched room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) aroused great interest. However, the dynamic control of RTP with a color-tunable persistent afterglow by external stimuli is still challenging. Herein, an appealing strategy for constructing dynamic hydrogen-bond networks based on boron-doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) was proposed to generate sequence-dependent stimuli-responsive RTP. The BCQDs exhibited bright RTP in paper matrix after successive stimulation by water and heat, demonstrating a fascinating regulation based on an AND logic gate. The RTP generated experienced a reversible switching without attenuation fatigue when BCQDs were heated and exposed to air. The switching of hydrogen-bond network from that among BCQDs to that between BCQDs and paper could facilitate the population of triplet-state BCQDs. The RTP can last a long timie of 10 s after the ceasation of excitation light source. Furthermore, the AND logic gate stimuli-responsive RTP with different colors in papers were obtainded for the first time after blending with various non-RTP dyes. The BCQDs with controllable and on-demand afterglow were further applied for advanced multi-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting materials. The finding provided assistance to understand the origin and mechanism of the stimuli-responsive RTP of smart materials and offered opportunities for developing multiple continuous stimuli-responsive intelligent RTP materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stimuli-responsive luminescence
Carbon quantum dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Information encryption
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the BCQDs and dyes mixed solution inks
Preparation of PVA-boric acid esterified BCQDs (PVA-BCQDs BAE)
Preparation of PVA-BCQDs and PVA-BCQDs BAE film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the overall fabrication process and the RTP sequential double-lock strategy generated by BCQDs for dynamic anti-counterfeiting.

Characterisation of the BCQDs. TEM image (a) and HRTEM image (b) of BCQDs. (c) Size distribution of BCQDs. (d) XRD patterns of BCQDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of BCQDs. (f) XPS survey spectrum of BCQDs. Deconvolved curves of the C 1 s (g), B 1 s (h) and O 1 s (i) XPS spectrum of BCQDs.

Photophysical properties of BCQDs aqueous solution coated paper. (a) Excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectra of BCQDs aqueous solution upon excitation at different wavelengths. (b) Phosphorescence spectra of the BCQDs aqueous solution coated paper after being heated to dry, and excitation at different wavelengths. (c) Phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of the BCQDs-coated paper. (d) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the BCQDs-coated paper. (e) DSC curve of the BCQDs aqueous solution coated-paper after being heated to dry. (f) Absorption spectrum of BCQDs aqueous solution.

RTP properties of BCQDs ethanol solution coated paper. (a) The phosphorescence spectra of the as-synthesized BCQDs, paper, and the BCQDs ethanol solution coated paper after being treated with heating, water or cascade water and heating, Ex = 254 nm. (b) Phosphorescence lifetime of the BCQDs-coated paper under excitation at 254 nm. (c) Phosphorescence spectra of the solid state BCQDs in vacuum with different temperatures under excitation at 254 nm. (d) Corresponding photographs of solid BCQDs and the BCQDs ethanol solution coated paper after being treated with cascade water and heating under daylight, excitation of 254 nm UV lamp and the cease of excitation.

Applications of cascade stimulus-responsive RTP materials. Photographs of the BCQDs ethanol solution coated paper with water and heat responsive phosphorescence in advanced anti-counterfeiting applications. The decryption process of confidential information (panda (a, c) and text (b, d)) through cascade water and heating treatment. (e) Mark “A” on BCQDs-coated filter paper with glass rod to test its application potential as scratch detection material. Scale bar: 1.5 cm.

Description of color-tunable cascade stimulus-responsive afterglow materials for multilevel anti-counterfeiting. (a) RTP photographs of dichlorofluorescein (d-fluorescein), rhodamine B (RhB), rhodamine 6G (RhB 6G) and rose bengal (RB) coated on filter paper in the absence and presence of BCQDs, the paper was treated with cascade water and heating. (b) The schematic illustration of the application process of BCQDs mixed with four commercial fluorescent dyes for anti-counterfeiting. Scale bar: 2.0 cm.

Cascade stimuli-responsive RTP system. Schematic representation of the changes in intermolecular interactions after the treatment with water or heating and the anti-counterfeiting application of BCQDs-coated paper with the water- and heating-responsive RTP, and the turn-on RTP after AND logic-gate successive stimulation of water and heating.

head.ce:title: A sequential dual-lock strategy for generation of room-temperature phosphorescence of boron doped carbon dots for dynamic anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology, College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Natural Products & Chemical Biology, College of Chemistry & Pharmacy, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimuli-responsive materials with dynamically switched room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) aroused great interest. However, the dynamic control of RTP with a color-tunable persistent afterglow by external stimuli is still challenging. Herein, an appealing strategy for constructing dynamic hydrogen-bond networks based on boron-doped carbon quantum dots (BCQDs) was proposed to generate sequence-dependent stimuli-responsive RTP. The BCQDs exhibited bright RTP in paper matrix after successive stimulation by water and heat, demonstrating a fascinating regulation based on an AND logic gate. The RTP generated experienced a reversible switching without attenuation fatigue when BCQDs were heated and exposed to air. The switching of hydrogen-bond network from that among BCQDs to that between BCQDs and paper could facilitate the population of triplet-state BCQDs. The RTP can last a long timie of 10 s after the ceasation of excitation light source. Furthermore, the AND logic gate stimuli-responsive RTP with different colors in papers were obtainded for the first time after blending with various non-RTP dyes. The BCQDs with controllable and on-demand afterglow were further applied for advanced multi-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting materials. The finding provided assistance to understand the origin and mechanism of the stimuli-responsive RTP of smart materials and offered opportunities for developing multiple continuous stimuli-responsive intelligent RTP materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Anti-counterfeiting and data security play a vital role in many fields including the economy, military, and daily applications [1]. Molecular persistent luminescent materials such as room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials with a visible afterglow have been widely used for encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications because they have low background interference, high contrast, large Stokes shifts, and long lifetimes compared with traditional fluorescent materials [2–4]. RTP emission originates from the unique spin-forbidden radiation transition process of intersystem crossing (ISC) from the singlet (S1) to triplet (T1) excited states [5]. To obtain RTP materials, there are two crucial factors for maintaining the stability of the triplet excited state to facilitate the efficient ISC process and suppress non-radiative decay [6]. Generally, RTP materials can be readily obtained by rigidifying the triplet excited states [7]. This was accomplished by embedding RTP substrates in rigid matrices/polymers to limit the vibrational and rotational suppression of the non-radiative transitions of triplet excited states [8]. Compared with RTP materials with static responses, those with stimuli-responsive (light, heat, stress, acid/alkali, oxygen, solvent vapor, etc.) changes could provide more monitoring parameters in addition to luminescence wavelength and lifetimes [9–11]. This is useful for high-level anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications because the materilas with dynamically responsive RTP are hard to replicate by the counterparts. However, materials with multiple stimulus-responsive RTP properties have been rarely reported. In the limited-number examples reported of stimulus-responsive RTP materials mainly focused on regulating the sensitive factors of the triplet state excitons [12–15]. However, due to the susceptible nature of the triplet state during the ISC process of producing phosphorescence and radiative decay, both single stimulus or multiple stimuli frequently lead to suppressing RTP. Besides, other adverse factors in these materials such as high background signals, low contrast, and inferior security impede the anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications of these RTP materials.

Compared to stimulus-responsive materials, the higher-level ones that revealing the sequence-dependent multiple stimulus-responsive RTP are more appealing. Recently, multiple sequential-stimulation materials have been extensively investigated in fields such as highly-sensitive analytical assays and intelligent nano-drug controlled-release platforms [9,16–18]. Continuous stimulation confers materials with ultra-high selectivity and precision. This strategy is similar to a combination lock that requires the correct keys (input stimulus) and input sequence to “unlock” the detection signal or control drug release [19–21]. This can avoid false-positive signals and the non-specific activation of drug release [22]. Despite the considerable success of this strategy in the field of analytical detection and drug release, the development of this strategy for stimuli-responsive RTP materials with a definite logical sequence has not yet been reported. Therefore, the exploration of stimuli-responsive turn-on RTP materials, especially those that can be activated by sequence-dependent multiple external stimuli for high-level anti-counterfeiting and encryption, is important but challenging.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional functional materials that have attracted considerable research attention because of their tunable excited state properties including luminescent components, adjustable emission range, rich energy levels, good biocompatibility, low cost, simple preparation techniques, and other advantages [23]. After the high-temperature carbonization of precursor molecules, nano-sized CQDs containing rich hydrogen bonding active sites on the internal cross-linked framework and external surface were formed [24]. The formation of hydrogen bonds among CQDs and/or between CQDs and a matrix can stabilize the triplet state and inhibit the non-radiative transition of CQDs. This enables the CQDs to provide RTP [25]. However, the strong hydrogen bond interactions between CQDs and the matrix make the triplet state of CQDs insensitive to external stimuli [26–28]. As a result, the RTP of these doped CQDs is “always-on” and does not have multi-stimuli responsivity. Therefore, to obtain high-level anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications, CQDs should possess suitable hydrogen bonding interactions with a matrix. Theoretically, the fine-tuning of hydrogen bonding interactions between CQDs and the matrix can be realized by reasonably designing the hydrogen bond active sites on the surface of CQDs. However, the effectiveness of existing strategies to adjust the hydrogen bonding interactions between CQDs and a matrix is still limited [29]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to develop new methods to obtain smart CQDs that can produce turn-on RTP after switching the hydrogen bonding interactions between CQDs and a matrix via multiple external stimuli for high-level anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications.

Herein, we report a strategy to construct a dynamic hydrogen-bonding network based on B-doped CQDs (BCQDs) to obtain multi-stimuli-responsive RTP, in which bright RTP was generated from BCQDs in paper matrix after successive stimulation by water and heat. In detail, the B-doping in CQDs reduced the singlet–triplet energy gap (ΔE ST), which promoted the production of more triplet excitons [30]. In addition, the electronegativity of B is much weaker than that of N, O, and F, and boric acid can self-crosslink to produce borate ester. All of these unique characteristics enable no additional hydrogen bond cross-linking with matrix, preventing BCQDs from forming a stable triplet state to elicit RTP before applying external stimuli [31]. Because the surface of the BCQDs contains abundant hydroxyl groups, the strong hydrogen bond networks between BCQDs would prevent BCQDs from forming hydrogen bonds with paper, rendering BCQDs unable to produce RTP. Water molecules broke the hydrogen bonds and borate bonds between BCQDs, and in turn formed new hydrogen-bonding networks between BCQDs and the paper matrix. After heating to remove free water and oxygen (the typical quenchers of triplet excitations), BCQDs in papers showed a noticeable afterglow with a large Stokes shift of nearly 200 nm, an ultra-long lifetime of 1.16 s, and the afterglow duration observed by the naked eye was about 10 s. Taking advantage of the good water solubility and unique AND logic gate continuous stimuli (water and heating) response characteristics, BCQDs realized the storage of confidential data and anti-counterfeiting of specified pattern (Scheme 1 and Fig. 1 ). Moreover, the RTP of BCQDs was completely reversible and silent after being treated alternately by heating and exposure to air, which further increased the anti-counterfeiting security and made it difficult to imitate. By introducing other commercial fluorescent dyes, such as fluorescein and rhodamine B, to the paper, the stimulus–response multiple-color afterglows were observed. The strategy for generation of RTP of boron doped carbon dots through multi-stimuli-responsive mechanism offered opportunities for developing multiple continuous stimuli-responsive intelligent RTP materials for dynamic anti-counterfeiting.

BCQDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, phenylboronic acid (0.2 mol) was dissolved in 50.0 mL ultrapure water under agitation to form a homogeneous solution. After vortex-mixing, the reaction solution was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and incubated at 240 °C for 10 h. After that, the solution was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained colorless solution was purified by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and dialysis (MWCO: 1000 Da) to remove the deposition and unreacted substance. The solid-state BCQDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying in vacuum. Finally, the resulting BCQDs were dispersed in ethanol as a stock solution for further characterization and test.

BCQDs were dispersed in ethanol via ultra-sonication to form a uniform solution (BCQDs: 4.0 mg/mL). This solution was used as printing ink (hereafter referred to as BCQDs ink) and injected into a vacant cartridge of a commercial ink-jet printer (HP deskjet 1112 printer). The predesigned patterns or words were printed onto a piece of commercial filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the BCQDs and dyes mixed solution inks
ce:section.ce:para: BCQDs and dyes were seperatedly dispersed in ethanol via ultra-sonication to form a uniform solution (BCQDs: 4.0 mg/mL, four dyes: 0.1 mmol). The solutions was used as spray-printing inks and injected into a watering can. The predesigned patterns of rose were spray-printed onto a piece of commercial filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA-boric acid esterified BCQDs (PVA-BCQDs BAE)
ce:section.ce:para: Boric acid esterified BCQDs (BCQDs BAE) was synthesized according to the literature with some modification [32]. In brief, BCQDs and pinacol (mass ratio 1:1) were dissolved in tetrahydrofuran including 4 Å molecular sieves. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h, and then the solvent was evaporated in vacuo, the obtained solid PVA-BCQDs BAE was redissolved in absolute ethanol for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA-BCQDs and PVA-BCQDs BAE film
ce:section.ce:para: 500 mg PVA was added into 3.0 mL ultrapure water. The solution was stirred at 80 0C for 20 min, then 0.7 mL PVA aqueous solution, was dropped on the cover glass with a pipette. The obtained glass was heated until water on it was evaporated off to obtain PVA film [33]. BCQDs or BCQDs BAE ethanol solution (4.0 mg/mL) was added dropwise on the PVA film above, then the film was dried at 45 °C to obtain PVA-BCQDs or PVA-BCQDs BAE film.

BCQDs were synthesized by using phenylboronic acid as the precursor via a one-step hydrothermal strategy. The morphology of the BCQDs was visualized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). It could be observed that the as-prepared BCQDs had a well-dispersed spheroidal structure with an average size of about 2.1 nm (Fig. 2 a and c). The HRTEM image revealed a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm in BCQDs, which was assigned to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b) [34]. As shown in Fig. 2d, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of BCQDs showed two diffraction peaks at 2θ = 24.8° and 28.2°, which revealed the general carbon nature of the BCQDs and confirmed the successful doping of B [35,36]. The surface chemical structures and chemical compositions of the BCQDs were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 2e, the absorption bands at 3212, 1604, 1440, 1349, 1189, and 1098 cm−1 corresponded to O H, CO, B–C, COC, and B–O groups/bonds, respectively [37,38]. The FT-IR spectroscopy results were further supported by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The BCQDs contained C, B, and O elements with percentages of 49.38 %, 23.5 %, and 27.12 %, respectively (Fig. 2f). The presence of C–B (283.7 eV), CC/CC (284.4 eV), C–OH/COC (285.3 eV), B–O and B–C (193.3 eV), C–OH/COC (532.1 eV), and B − O (533.4 eV) was confirmed by the high-resolution XPS spectra of the BCQDs (Fig. 2g-i) [38,39]. These XPS results were in good accordance with the FT-IR analysis, demonstrating that the synthesized BCQDs contained many –OH groups and B heteroatoms.

To study the optical properties of the BCQDs, their fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the BCQDs displayed excitation-independent fluorescence emission, implying that the synthesized BCQDs have a pure surface state and uniform size distribution. The maximum emission wavelength was found at 302 nm under 270 nm excitation. After dropping the BCQDs aqueous solution onto filter paper, and then heating and drying the resulting paper and ceasing irradiation by the excitation light, a cyan afterglow was observed from the BCQDs-coated paper by the naked eye, which lasted for nearly 10 s (Fig. S1). The phosphorescence wavelength of the BCQDs-coated paper was excitation-independent, and the phosphorescence emission intensity at 480 nm reached a maximum under excitation at 285 nm (Fig. 3b and c), and the large Stokes shift close to 200 nm indicated that the luminescence had no delayed fluorescence (Fig. 3d). The BCQDs did not emit phosphorescence in the air, while the BCQDs-coated paper did, which may be due to the synergistic interactions between BCQDs and the paper matrix. The phosphorescence quantum yields (Φ) of BCQDs powder and BCQDs-coated paper are measured about close to zero and 0.15 %, respectively. To verify the presence of hydrogen bonds, we tested the exothermic curve of BCQDs-coated paper by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). As shown in Fig. 3e, two exothermic transitions at 10–40 °C and 119 °C were observed, suggesting that hydrogen bonds between the matrix (paper) and BCQDs [40], which was conducive to stabilizing the triplet excited state to elicit RTP.

To search for the source of phosphorescence, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the BCQDs were measured. The absorption spectrum of BCQDs showed a strong absorption band at 240–300 nm (Fig. 3f), which was attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds [38,41]. As shown in Fig. 3c and f, the excitation wavelength of the phosphorescence of BCQDs appeared near 285 nm, which greatly overlapped with the 255 nm absorption peak of BCQDs. Therefore, the π-π* transition of CC bonds was considered to be one of the origins of phosphorescence [42]. It has been reported that paper, whose main component is cellulose, has weak phosphorescence at 400–600 nm [43]. To exclude this phosphorescence, the phosphorescence spectra of untreated paper and cellulose were tested. As shown in Fig. S2a and b, paper and cellulose both had weak excitation-dependent phosphorescence, which was completely different from the strong excitation-independent phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper, proving that the phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper comes from BCQDs rather than the paper components. To gain further insight into the phosphorescence mechanism of the BCQDs-coated paper, the phosphorescence spectra of solid BCQDs powder at different temperatures in vacuum were tested. As shown in Fig. 4 c, under different temperatures, the phosphorescence intensity of BCQDs powder at 490 nm in vacuum gradually decreased with the increase of temperature, which further indicated that BCQDs did not produce delayed fluorescence, and also confirmed that the generating RTP of BCQDs-coated paper was originate from BCQDs. Phosphorescence ΔE ST of BCQDs powder in vacuum was 1.46 eV, while that of BCQDs coated paper was 1.42 eV, indicating that the hydrogen bonding environment provided by the paper could effectively reduce ΔE ST to facilitate the ISC process. In addition, phosphorescence occurred only after the BCQDs were coated on paper and when the resulting paper was heated and dried. According to the above analysis, it is presumed that the paper matrix promoted the rigidity of BCQDs to minimize the molecular motion and rotation of BCQDs, resulting in RTP emission.

Since an ethanol solution of BCQDs was used instead of an aqueous one, no afterglow was observed from the treated paper under identical conditions (Fig. 4a), which implied that water played an important role in the production of RTP. Compared with ethanol, water has a stronger hydrogen bonding affinity, which can breach the existing weak hydrogen bonds and also form new hydrogen bonds with many atoms (O, N, heteroatoms) or functional groups (–OH, –NH2, –COOH, etc.) [44–47]. In the absence of water, the boric acid moieties on the BCQDs self-assembled into borate esters, and the cellulose of paper underwent self-cross-linking through hydrogen bonds. The borate esters and hydrogen bonds between BCQDs hindered the formation of hydrogen bonds between BCQDs and paper, which did not help stabilize the excited triplet excitons to achieve RTP. After the addition of water, the water prevented the hydrolysis of borate esters and broke the hydrogen bonds between cellulose chains. As the hydroxyl groups on the surface of BCQDs were exposed, some formed new borate esters with the hydroxyl groups of cellulose in paper. In addition, water behaved as a bridge in the hydrogen bond network to connect carbon dots with the paper matrix. The newly-formed borate esters and hydrogen bond networks effectively stabilized the triplet state of BCQDs, minimizing the vibration and rotation of BCQDs, and inhibiting non-radiative decay, which is a crucial prerequisite for producing RTP. Heating further removed free water and air in paper to prevent phosphorescence quenching by unbound water and oxygen in the air. The presence of hydrogen bonds was confirmed by analyzing the surface elements and compositions of the BCQDs using XPS and infrared spectra, respectively. After the BCQDs ethanol solution was coated on paper and then dried by heating, the BCQDs-coated paper showed no obvious endothermic transitions. However, after being treated with water and then dried, the DSC curve of the BCQDs ethanol solution-coated paper showed two obvious endothermic transitions at about 10–40 °C and 119 °C, respectively, which corresponded to the destruction of hydrogen bonds between BCQDs and the formation of hydrogen bonds between paper and BCQDs ( Fig. S7a). The strong and wide stretching vibration peak near 3300 cm−1 was attributed to the hydrogen bonds in the FT-IR spectrum of BCQDs-coated paper (Fig. S7b) increased, which also indicates the formation of hydrogen bonds between BCQDs and paper after water treatment [48].

The phosphorescence intensity was related to the water content added. At least 20 v% (water/ethanol) was needed before phosphorescence could be detected from the BCQDs-coated paper (Fig. S3a and b). After ultrapure water was added, the obtained phosphorescence intensity was the highest with long lifetime of 1.16 s and a cyan afterglow nearly 10 s (Fig. 4b and d). These results demonstrate that the resulting RTP was directly related to water stimulation. It is well known that H2O2 can destroy borate esters [49], so the effect of H2O2 on the phosphorescence of BCQDs-coated paper was investigated to verify the contribution of newly formed borate esters between BCQDs and matrix paper. As shown in Fig. S4a and b, H2O2 quickly annihilated the phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper, which means that borate esters formed between the paper and BCQDs [50].

To further verify the formation of hydrogen bonds and borate esters between BCQDs and paper, easily characterized polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was used instead of paper. For compasion, the boric acid groups on the BCQDs were pre-esterified by pinacol to form borate ester to achieve BCQDs BAE, which would prevent BCQDs, to a great extent, from further interacting with PVA via hydrogen bonds and borate esters interactions. Similar to the synthesis of PVA-BCQDs film, PVA-BCQDs BAE film was conducted. As shown in Fig. S5, PVA, PVA-BCQDs film and PVA-BCQDs BAE film showed no RTP emission. After treatment with continuous water and heat, obvious RTP was observed from PVA-BCQDs film and PVA-BCQDs BAE film, demonstrating that BCQDs possess the stimuli-responsive RTP chanracteristic in PVA film same as that in paper. Compared with PVA-BCQDs film, PVA-BCQDs BAE film showed weaker RTP intensity and shorter lifetime of RTP due to the boric acid groups of BCQDs were shielded, which confirmed the formation of boronic acid ester between the boronic acid group on BCQDs and the hydroxyl group on PVA is important for generation of RTP [33].

The peak at 3267 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of PVA-BCQDs film red-shifted to 3300 cm−1 after continuous water and heating treatment (Fig. S6a), which confirmed the formation of hydrogen bond between PVA and BCQDs [33]. Meanwhile, a new absorption peak at 1320 cm−1 ascribed to the B-O bond occurred. The same absorption peak was also found in PVA-BCQDs BAE film, which confirmed that the formation of boronate esters between PVA and BCQDs (Fig. S6c) [51]. The absorption band of PVA-BCQDs film at 265.4 nm red-shifted to 271.7 nm after continuous water and heat treatment, which was consistent with the PVA-BCQDs BAE film having boronate esters (Fig. S6b and d) [33].

As expected, BCQDs BAE-coated paper illustrated weak and short-lived RTP after treatment with water and heating as compared with BCQDs-coated paper. The above results demonstrated that the stimulus-responsiveness was derived from the water-induced hydrogen bonding between BCQDs and paper and the formation of borate esters [52,53]. The phosphorescence of BCQDs-coated paper comes from the combined effects of hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds formed between BCQDs and paper on the triplet state of BCQDs.

The BCQDs ethanol solution-coated paper showed strong RTP emission under ambient conditions after being treated with successive water and heating, but its RTP emission was gradually attenuated in the air (Fig. S7c and d). The disappeared RTP could be recovered by re-heating the paper. To clarify the contribution of heating in the RTP system, the phosphorescence spectra and lifetimes of the BCQDs ethanol solution-coated paper in two groups were measured. The paper in both groups was treated with water and then dried to remove free water and placed in the air until the RTP disappeared. One group was reheated and the other was degassed in vacuum, while the RTP spectra and lifetime curves of the two groups at room temperature were highly consistent, indicating that heating removed oxygen in the paper to reduce the quenching effect of oxygen on the phosphorescence of BCQDs. Except for the removal of oxygen, heating also removed free water because there was no RTP after the paper was placed in a vacuum in the presence of unbound water. To systematically evaluate the effect of heating on RTP, the RTP intensity of BCQDs-coated paper after heating at different temperatures was measured. The RTP intensity increased significantly upon increasing the temperature starting from 100 °C and reached the highest at 120 °C. When the temperature increased to 140 °C, the phosphorescence intensity of the BCQDs-coated paper decreased significantly because a high temperature promoted non-radiative transitions and weakened the phosphorescence (Fig. S3c and d) [15].

To eliminate the possibility that luminescence originated from the thermally-activated delayed fluorescent (TADF), the phosphorescence spectra and lifetimes of the BCQDs-coated paper at different temperatures were tested. As shown in Fig. S3e and f, as the temperature gradually increased from 77 K to 400 K, the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of BCQDs-coated paper decreased significantly, excluding the existence of TADF [54]. In addition, it was inferred that the heating area on the paper was hypoxic, which thermally activated RTP. Due to the shielding effect of hydrogen bond networks between BCQDs and paper, the anaerobic environment of the BCQDs provided by heating once produced RTP that lasted about 30 min (Fig. S7d). The RTP of BCQDs-coated paper was inactivated after ambient oxygen penetrated the paper again. The alternate thermal activation and air annihilation of the RTP of BCQDs-coated paper could be repeated many times without significant attenuation of the RTP (Fig. S7c), demonstrating good resistance to RTP fatigue. This reversible stimulus response makes it possible to design and develop intelligent materials that can be used to hide anti-counterfeiting information.

Through detailed analysis, it was concluded that the phosphorescence originated from the triplet state of BCQDs (Scheme 1). The addition of water induced BCQDs to form hydrogen bond and covalent bond networks with paper, and heating removed free water and oxygen from paper, demonstrating an AND logic gate based stimuli-responsive RTP. The hydrogen bond and covalent bond networks between BCQDs and paper limited the vibration and rotation of the surface groups of BCQDs and stabilized the triplet state of BCQDs, which boosted efficient ISC. Minimizing the non-radiative transition due to the rigid environment constructed from hydrogen-bond networks and the removal of free water and oxygen promoted the occurrence of RTP. Smart materials with tandem and sequence-dependent dual-stimuli-responsive RTP have great potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

To evaluate the RTP stability of the BCQDs-coated paper, the effect of water with different pH values was studied. As shown in Fig. S8, after cessation of UV irradiation, the phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper remained unchanged under the stimulation of water with a pH from 2 to 10, indicating that the RTP of the BCQDs-coated paper had good stability against strong acids and bases. In addition, the phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper could only be turned on by water and not by common organic solvents. It was also unaffected by the components in water, such as metal ions, anions, and physiological molecules, suggesting that water is a specific and selective stimulus that triggers the RTP production of BCQDs-coated paper. Overall, the phosphorescence of BCQDs-coated paper had significant stability and water-responsive selectivity, showing its application potential in complex systems.

Based on the cascade water and heating-responsive luminescence and high stability and stimuli selectivity of RTP, a BCQDs ethanol solution was used as an advanced encryption security ink for multi-level encryption and anti-counterfeiting. These smart materials with dual-stimuli and sequence-dependent turn-on RTP are very rare and difficult to replicate. Taking BCQDs ethanol solution as the ink, various complex patterns including text and paintings were printed directly on filter paper (Fig. 1). The paper was thoroughly dried under ambient conditions for applications. Because BCQDs were colorless and their weak emission was obscured by the background emission of paper, their fluorescence was not activated; thus, the printed encrypted information could not be parsed under sunlight, UV irradiation, or even after ceasing UV irradiation. To accurately identify the encrypted information, water spraying and heating were applied to the system for decryption. As shown in Fig. 5 a–d, only after the correct treatment sequence with water and heating could the encrypted information (text and panda) printed with BCQDs ethanol solution be observed with high-resolution RTP after terminating UV excitation. However, the proper information could not be read in the wrong processing mode, for example, with separate water or heating treatments, or incorrect sequence of heating first and then water (Fig. S9). Moreover, due to the quenching effect of oxygen in the air, the phosphorescence of the printed pattern on the paper was automatically lost after removing the heat source and exposing it to the air for 30 min. The functionality of air-mediated loss of phosphorescence improved the information safety in the printed pattern. Interestingly, after reheating the paper, the information could be read again, and the thermal activation and air quenching processes were reversible, which proved that BCQDs have excellent stability and repeatability for multiple information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

The RTP of BCQDs-coated paper could be re-activated by simply removing air, while a scratch on the paper squeezed out the air in the scratched area, enabling the hypoxic area to generate RTP. It was envisioned that the BCQDs-coated paper could be used to detect scratches/stress on paper. The paper coated with BCQDs was exposed to air until its RTP was completely inactivated, then the English letter “A” was scratched on the paper with a glass rod. As shown in Fig. 5e, no scratches were detected under ambient light. However, a cyan afterglow of “A” on the scratched area was noticeable on the paper. The BCQDs-coated paper produced cyan RTP under mechanical force, which makes it promising as a scratch or stress-detection material. For practical applications, good biocompatibility is a crucial factor. The cytotoxicity of BCQDs was studied by MTT tests to evaluate their biocompatibility. As shown in Fig. S10, the cell survival rate exceeded 90 %, even in the presence of 500 µg/mL BCQDs, indicating that these BCQDs showed low cytotoxicity. The excellent biocompatibility illustrated the feasibility and great potential commercial applications of the BCQDs as an anti-counterfeiting ink for paper documents, trademarks, bar code labels, and paintings.

To further expand the versatility and diversity of the cascade water and thermal stimuli-responsive RTP, a triplet-to-singlet Förster resonance energy transfer (TS-FRET) strategy was leveraged to yield persistent afterglow with different colors [55–57]. Specifically, BCQDs were used as long-lived energy donors, and four commercially available fluorescent dyes not possessing persistent luminescence, e.g., dichlorofluorescein (d-fluorescein), rhodamine B (RhB), rhodamine 6G (RhB 6G), and rose Bengal (RB), were selected as energy acceptors. The Förster energy transfer from the triplet state of BCQDs to the singlet state of the fluorescent dye was expected to generate on-demand color-tunable afterglow. The UV–vis absorption spectra of these four commercial dyes overlap well with the phosphorescence spectrum of the BCQDs-coated paper (Fig. S12a), satisfying the prerequisite for TS-FRET. The reduced luminescence intensity (around 320 nm) of the BCQDs-coated paper and remarkably enhanced fluorescence intensity of the dyes after the four dyes were added to the paper (Fig. S12b-f) indicated that there was a strong TS-FRET between the BCQDs and the fluorescent dyes (Fig. S11). The four fluorescent dyes-coated paper or even after cascaded treatment with water and heating cannot produce afterglow (Fig. 6 a). Different from the cyan afterglow of the BCQDs-coated paper, the paper pretreated with the ethanol mixed solution of BCQDs and dyes elicited different colors of afterglow from green to blue purple after cascaded water and heating treatment (Fig. S13). The triplet energy transfer of BCQDs to the dye acceptor enables the dyes to acquire the ability of emitting afterglow. As a result, smart materials with cascaded water and heating stimuli responses and tunable afterglow colors in a wide range can be conveniently achieved, which is beneficial to expand their practical applications.

Taking advantage of the color-tunable afterglow upon the stimulation, BCQDs-dyes as inks were spray-printed a rose on filter paper. Different parts (flowers, leaves, branches) were daubed with BCQDs and corresponding dyes ethanol solution. The specific spray-printing scheme was shown in Fig. 6b. Under UV light excitation, the roses printed by these different printing schemes showed multi-color fluorescence, and a rose pattern with multi-color afterglow appeared after the UV light was turned off. It was worth noting that the BCQDs-dye-coated paper perfectly inherited the cascade water and heating stimuli-response properties of the BCQDs-coated paper, and the wrong sequence of adding stimuli would not turn on the afterglow of pattern (Fig. S14). The multiple afterglow colors enrich the monitoring parameters in addition to the cascade stimulus response, which further improves the security level of anti-counterfeiting.

In conclusion, we designed and developed boron-doped BCQDs to construct stimuli-responsive intelligent RTP materials. The BCQDs with a lifetime of 10 s after turning off the excitation light source exhibited bright RTP in a paper matrix when successive stimulation by water and heat. The RTP could be easily viewed by a conventional camera or the naked eye. The phosphorescence of the BCQDs-coated paper showed good reversibility after alternate heating and exposure to air. Advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting based on the BCQDs' unique continuous and sequence-dependent water and heating-sensitive RTP emission were realized, which showed that the BCQDs ethanol dispersion be used as intelligent security ink for multi-level security applications. It was found that the dynamic hydrogen bond and covalent bond networks between BCQDs and cellulose in the paper were important factors for obtaining stimulus-responsive RTP. This may provide a new idea for the design of multi-stimuli-responsive phosphorescent materials. Through TS-FRET between the BCQDs and common fluorescnent dyes, tunable afterglow color from blue to green to purple was achieved. It is believed that the strategy of tunable hydrogen bond and covalent bond networks between BCQDs and a matrix can provide new opportunities for the design of a variety of stimulus-responsive RTP materials with tunable afterglow colors and applications in the field of information security and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21978241, 21878249, U1803283 and 21676218). This work is dedicataed to Prof. Xiaojun Peng on the occasion of his 60th birthday.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.11.062.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
